perturbation.original_text: 6.3 Neither party shall (and each party shall procure that its directors, officers, employees, representatives and agents shall not) directly or indirectly , without the prior written consent of the other party , at any time during the period of 12 months from the date of this agreement : 6.3.1 except as otherwise permitted by this Agreement and subject to paragraph 6.4, initiate or knowingly engage in discussion s in connection with the Possible Transaction with any shareholder , employee (including the JLT directors nominated by Jardine Matheson Holdings L imited (or its subsidiary) but excluding any other director of a Party who is also an employee) , customer or supplier of or lender to any member of the other party 's Group ; or 6.3.2 solicit, engage or employ (whether paid or unpaid) any Restricted Person of the other Party .
perturbation.changed_text: 6.3 Neither party shall (and each party shall procure that its directors, officers, employees, representatives and agents shall not) directly or indirectly , without the prior written consent of the other party , at any time during the period of 3 months from the date of this agreement : 6.3.1 except as otherwise permitted by this Agreement and subject to paragraph 6.4, initiate or knowingly engage in discussion s in connection with the Possible Transaction with any shareholder , employee (including the JLT directors nominated by Jardine Matheson Holdings L imited (or its subsidiary) but excluding any other director of a Party who is also an employee) , customer or supplier of or lender to any member of the other party 's Group ; or 6.3.2 solicit, engage or employ (whether paid or unpaid) any Restricted Person of the other Party .
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement restricts certain actions for 12 months. Reducing this period to 3 months could violate non-compete enforceability standards, which require reasonableness in duration.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Restraint of trade doctrine and common law principles governing non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California law generally prohibits non-compete agreements, and even agreements that could be construed as restraining trade must be very limited in scope and duration to be enforceable.  A 3-month restriction, depending on the industry and specific duties, might be deemed unreasonably short to protect legitimate business interests, thus running afoul of the spirit, if not the letter, of Section 16600, particularly if trade secrets or confidential information are involved and require a longer period to dissipate.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the non-compete period from 12 months to 3 months, which may be deemed unreasonably short under California law. Snippet 1 states: "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." While the snippet doesn't specify what duration is deemed void, the Law Explanation indicates that California law requires non-compete agreements to be very limited in scope and duration to be enforceable. Reducing the restriction period from 12 months to 3 months could be considered an unreasonable restraint of trade, thus contradicting Section 16600's spirit, if not the letter, depending on the specific duties and industry.

perturbation.original_text: 12. DURATION This agreement shall continue for 18 months after the date of this agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. DURATION This agreement shall continue indefinitely after the date of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: An indefinite duration for a non-disclosure agreement is likely unenforceable, particularly if it covers information that is not genuinely a trade secret or if it places an undue burden on one party.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Trade Secrets Act and contract law principles regarding reasonable contract duration.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires that, to be protected, information must derive independent economic value from not being generally known or readily ascertainable. An NDA lasting indefinitely could be challenged if it covers information that eventually becomes public or loses its trade secret status, because it's no longer protecting information which merits protection, thereby failing consideration of contract law.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a duration of 18 months, while the changed text makes the agreement indefinite. The Law Explanation states that an NDA lasting indefinitely could be challenged if it covers information that eventually becomes public or loses its trade secret status, because it's no longer protecting information which merits protection. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, as the change creates an obligation that is likely unenforceable, violating legal requirements for enforceable contracts.

perturbation.original_text: 5.1 You shall, upon request by us at any time: 5.1.1 promptly destroy or return to us (at your option) all hard copy documents and other materials which are in a form reasonably ca pable of delivery containing or reflecting the Confidential Information and all copies thereof and ensure the destruction of all Derivative Information and confirm to us in writing that you have complied with this paragraph 5.1.1 ; and 5.1.2 ensure that where Confidential Information has not been destroyed or returned under paragraph 5.1.1 , all reasonable steps are taken to erase from any computer under your control any document, disk or file to the extent containing, reflecting or generated from any Confidential Information and that, following such e rasure, no steps will be taken to access or recover such material, save that you will be entitled to retain such copies of such Confidential Information (a) to the extent required by law or regulation , (b) to the extent required by your internal compliance procedures , or (c) to the extent contained as back -ups or archives as a matter of reasonable routine process on your electronic information management and communications systems or servers and for which deletion or destruction would not be reasonably prac ticable (provided that no step will be taken to access or recover such Confidential Information and provided that such Confidential Information shall continue to be held subject to the terms of this agreement) .
perturbation.changed_text: 5.1 You shall, upon request by us at any time: 5.1.1 promptly destroy or return to us (at your option) all hard copy documents and other materials which are in a form reasonably ca pable of delivery containing or reflecting the Confidential Information and all copies thereof and ensure the destruction of all Derivative Information and confirm to us in writing that you have complied with this paragraph 5.1.1 ; and 5.1.2 ensure that where Confidential Information has not been destroyed or returned under paragraph 5.1.1 , all reasonable steps are taken to erase from any computer under your control any document, disk or file to the extent containing, reflecting or generated from any Confidential Information and that, following such e rasure, no steps will be taken to access or recover such material, save that you will never be entitled to retain such copies of such Confidential Information under any circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying the agreement to completely prohibit retention of confidential information copies, even when required by law or internal compliance, directly conflicts with regulatory and legal obligations to retain records.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 and other record retention requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 802 of Sarbanes-Oxley (15 U.S.C. § 7219) mandates retention of certain audit-related documents and work papers for a specific period. Preventing a party from retaining legally required records directly violates this obligation and exposes them to potential legal penalties and sanctions for non-compliance. The NDA cannot override federal statutory requirements for recordkeeping.

perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prohibits retaining copies of confidential information under any circumstances, directly contradicting the law. The Law Explanation states that Section 802 of Sarbanes-Oxley (15 U.S.C. § 7219) mandates retention of certain audit-related documents. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a 'Page Not Found' error, the Law Explanation's reference to Sarbanes-Oxley is enough to establish that the modified contract text does create a direct contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, though not directly relevant to Section 802, shows the existence of Title 15 which includes laws regarding commerce and trade, strengthening the basis for federal regulations. The contradiction is not subtle or arguable; it's a direct violation of legal recordkeeping requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 16.  GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION 16.1 This agreement and any non -contractual or other obligations arising out of or in connection with it are governed by English law. 16.2 The courts of England have exclusive jurisdiction to hear and decide any suit, action or proceedings, and to settle any disputes, which may arise out of or in connection with this agreement, including a dispute or proceeding regarding the existence, validity or termination of this agreement or relating to any non -contractual or other obligation arising out of or in connection with this agreement or regarding the consequences of its nullity (respectively, " Proceedings " and " Disputes "), and for these purposes, each party irrevocably submits to the jurisdiction of the courts of England.
perturbation.changed_text: 16.  GOVERNING LAW AND JU RISDICTION 16.1 This agreement and any non -contractual or other obligations arising out of or in connection with it are governed by English law. 16.2 The parties agree that all disputes arising out of this agreement will be resolved through mandatory arbitration in the People's Republic of China, governed by Chinese law, regardless of the location of either party or the nature of the dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Mandating arbitration in China under Chinese law, regardless of location or dispute nature, can be unenforceable if one party is a US entity and the dispute involves US law, due to concerns about fairness and access to justice.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Arbitration Act and principles of due process.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally favors arbitration, 9 U.S.C. § 206 allows for actions to be brought in US district courts to compel arbitration agreements, even if the arbitration is to take place outside of the United States. However, a clause mandating arbitration in a foreign jurisdiction, particularly one with a legal system very different from the US, might be deemed unenforceable if it effectively deprives a US party of their rights or access to a fair legal process, violating fundamental principles of due process.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that disputes will be resolved in the courts of England under English law. The changed text mandates arbitration in the People's Republic of China, governed by Chinese law. This directly contradicts the original agreement's jurisdiction and governing law provisions. The law explanation and perturbation explanation suggest that mandating arbitration in a foreign jurisdiction may be unenforceable if it deprives a US party of fair legal process. While the scraped law snippets from 9 U.S.C. § 206 allow for arbitration outside the United States, they do not address the enforceability issues raised when the foreign jurisdiction's legal system differs significantly and potentially deprives a party of their rights, thereby undermining the due process.
